name: Al McKibbon
abstract: Al McKibbon (January 1, 1919 – July 29, 2005) was an American jazz double bassist, known for his work in bop, hard bop, and Latin jazz. In 1947, after working with Lucky Millinder, Tab Smith, J.

Carnegie Pop Concert: Jazz at the Philharmonic

works.t: No No Nanette: Tea for Two

concerts.label: Carnegie Pop Concert: Jazz at the Philharmonic

about_quotes.speaker: Gerald Wilson
about_quotes.evidence: Then you had Al McKibbon on bass... And myself, that was the trumpet section... Man, that was some band
about_quotes.doc_title: Gerald Wilson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Jones
about_quotes.evidence: We had a very good rhythm section with her. It was Al McKivven (PHONETIC), Kibbon, John Collins. I played piano, and Joe Jones on drums.

about_quotes.speaker: Big Nick Nicholas
about_quotes.evidence: Kelly Martin, it was a 12-piece band, and Al McKibbon was in the band... I think we worked there six or seven months.

about_quotes.speaker: Konitz Lee
about_quotes.evidence: The second week was with a quintet with me, Miles, John Lewis, Al McKibbon, and Max.
about_quotes.doc_title: Konitz Lee — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Gene Ramey
about_quotes.evidence: So Al McKibbon had to double. He had to play with Basie and with the band, so they called me from Denver and asked me -- I was working the regular man in Birdland, you know. They called me at Birdland and asked me if I'd come, you know. And I said, you make the price right, I'll come.

about_quotes.speaker: Wendell Brunious
about_quotes.evidence: Vladimir had a great Latin band, which he had like Al McKibbon and Don Alias and Bobby Rodriguez, and all these guys were in his band... And so we formed a Latin band in New Orleans... Vladimir and the Aquarians.
about_quotes.doc_title: Wendell Brunious interviewed by Monk Rowe, Scottsdale, Arizona, April 16, 2000 [interview 2 of 2]
